title: Graph Attention Networks (GAT)
summary: Apply attention mechanism to graph edges. Each node computes attention weights over its neighbors — not all neighbors contribute equally. Directly analogous to transformer self-attention, but only over graph neighbors instead of all tokens.
key_insights: GCN: all neighbors contribute equally (normalized by degree). GAT: learn different weights per neighbor via attention.
Attention: $\alpha_{ij} = \text{softmax}_j(\text{LeakyReLU}(a^\top [W h_i \| W h_j]))$. Learned query-key-like scoring.
Multi-head GAT: $K$ attention heads, concatenate or average outputs. Same idea as transformer multi-head.
Transformer self-attention = GAT where the graph is fully connected and attention uses dot-product instead of concat.
Sparse attention: GAT only computes attention over actual edges. $O(|E|)$ not $O(N^2)$. Efficient on sparse graphs.
Inductive: GAT generalizes to unseen nodes (attention weights depend on features, not node identity).

derivations.title: GAT vs Transformer attention — structural comparison

steps.name: GAT
steps.math: $\alpha_{ij} = \text{softmax}_j(a^\top [Wh_i \| Wh_j])$. Concat attention. Only neighbors ($|E|$ pairs).

steps.name: Key differences
steps.math: Transformer: $O(N^2)$ compute, dynamic edges. GAT: $O(|E|)$ compute, fixed edges from graph. Transformer can discover any relationship; GAT is constrained to graph structure.

derivations.consequence: GAT trades expressivity (fixed graph) for efficiency (sparse computation). Useful when graph structure encodes real constraints (molecules, social networks).

enables: MULTIMODAL.FUSION — graph attention for cross-modal reasoning
